video_title: The Strong Do What They Can and Suﬀer What They Must | Jonathan Kirshner
gist: Political scientist Jonathan Kirshner argues the real lesson of Thucydides's Peloponnesian War is that great powers are undone by their own hubris and restlessness, not by shifts in the balance of power, and applies that lens directly to the U.S., framing Iraq, Afghanistan, the 2008 financial crisis, and the current Iran strikes as expressions of unconstrained imperial overreach. He contends Trump-era foreign policy defies standard IR theory and realism and is better explained as a 'personalist kleptocracy,' warning that depleted Middle East munitions stockpiles and eroding alliance credibility are directly weakening the U.S. position against China in East Asia.

themes.theme: Reinterpreting Thucydides: Hubris, Not Power Transition

insights.claim: Graham Allison's 'Thucydides Trap' framework, which argues rising Athenian power provoked Spartan fear and caused the Peloponnesian War, rests on a shallow, decontextualized reading of Thucydides and misrepresents the historian's actual argument.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Investors and policymakers using the 'Thucydides Trap' as a framework for US-China conflict risk may be relying on a flawed historical analogy that overstates the inevitability of great-power war.
insights.quote: I'm going to be a little impolite and say it's not a very good book, but it isn't.

insights.claim: Kirshner argues the true lesson of the Peloponnesian War is that great powers are undone by their own arrogance and hubris rather than by shifts in the balance of power or rival competition.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Frames US strategic risk (Iraq, Afghanistan, Iran) as self-inflicted overreach rather than externally imposed threats, a useful lens for assessing sovereign and geopolitical risk.
insights.quote: great powers are not undermined or destroyed by shifts in the balance of power so much as they are undermined by their own arrogance.

insights.claim: The Melian Dialogue's famous line that 'the strong do what they will and the weak what they must' is not Thucydides's endorsement of might-makes-right realism, but a narrative device used to link Athenian arrogance at Melos to its catastrophic defeat in Sicily.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: The maxim commonly cited to justify power politics is frequently weaponized out of context by policymakers to rationalize coercive action, an interpretive error worth recognizing when officials invoke 'realist' rhetoric.
insights.quote: the strong do what they will and the weak what they must

insights.claim: Classical scholars link Athens's massacre at Melos to its immediately following, disastrous Sicilian expedition, arguing Thucydides intentionally paired an act of hubris abroad with catastrophic overreach as cause and effect.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Offers a historical pattern for reading whether an act of overwhelming coercive power (e.g., recent US strikes on Iran) foreshadows overextension in a subsequent, higher-stakes theater such as Asia.

themes.theme: The Political Logic of Restraint and Atrocity

insights.claim: In the Mytilenean debate, Diodotus persuaded Athens to spare a rebellious ally not on moral grounds but by arguing mass execution would destroy an important revenue stream and remove any incentive for future rebels to surrender rather than fight to the death.
insights.speaker: Demetri Kofinas, host (summarizing Thucydides, corroborated by Kirshner)
insights.so_what: A pure self-interest, non-moral argument against maximal coercion offers a template for evaluating whether harsh punitive strategies (sanctions, escalation) are self-defeating on narrow cost-benefit grounds alone.
insights.quote: destroying Mitilini would mean the loss of an important revenue stream for the Athenian Empire

insights.claim: Athenian leaders initially voted to massacre the rebellious city of Mytilene, then reversed the decision overnight after reflecting that the act was 'so horrid,' sending a second ship to countermand the order just in time.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Shows that even a coercive imperial power can pull back from maximal violence once political costs are made salient, relevant to gauging room for de-escalation once initial escalatory decisions face domestic scrutiny.
insights.quote: so horrid an act that they were contemplating

insights.claim: By contrast, when Athens destroyed Melos later in the war (year 16-17), it acted with no comparable moral hesitation, illustrating how prolonged war hardens a society's willingness to commit atrocities over time.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: A caution that protracted conflict tends to erode restraint the longer it persists, raising tail risk of escalation in extended modern engagements (Iraq/Afghanistan-style wars, or a prolonged Iran confrontation).
insights.quote: it was simply reflexively barbaric.

themes.theme: American Hubris: Iraq, Afghanistan, and the Financial Crisis

insights.claim: Kirshner's teacher Robert Gilpin predicted in 2003, before the Iraq War, that the conflict would have consequences for American society that had 'not even begun to be comprehended' at the time, and Kirshner argues this prediction proved correct via the broader hollowing out of American institutions over the following two decades.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner (citing Robert Gilpin)
insights.so_what: Suggests looking past direct war costs to longer-run domestic institutional decay as a channel through which foreign overreach damages a hegemon's underlying strength, relevant to US political-risk premia.
insights.quote: this war, which he thought was a ridiculous idea, will have consequences for the nature of American society that we have not even begun to comprehend.

insights.claim: Kirshner frames the 2008 Global Financial Crisis as another expression of American hubris, arguing the US 'hyperpower' of the late 1990s forced financial globalization on the world out of a self-assured belief it was correct, and that this arrogance contributed directly to the crisis.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Reframes the GFC as part of a recurring geopolitical hubris pattern rather than a purely technical regulatory failure, useful for thinking about future policy overreach preceding financial shocks.
insights.quote: We believe in financial global liberalization and we will impose it on you because we know it's the right thing.

insights.claim: Kirshner argues both the Iraq/Afghanistan wars and the Global Financial Crisis stem from the same root cause: American hubris exercised without the constraint of a peer competitor, exactly as Thucydides warned of unconstrained Athenian power.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Implies a genuine peer competitor (China) could act as a disciplining force on future US policy overreach, an argument for why great-power competition may reduce, not just raise, certain categories of geopolitical risk.

insights.claim: Kirshner links the absence of a nearby security threat directly to the scale of past US strategic mistakes, arguing a peer military competitor forces prioritization and tempers behavior.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: A contrarian macro read: rising China competition could paradoxically improve US strategic discipline and resource prioritization rather than simply raising conflict risk.
insights.quote: Having a peer military competitor can actually be very tempering of your behavior because you really have to prioritize what is actually important to you.

themes.theme: Iran Strikes and the Cost to America's China Pivot

insights.claim: Kirshner argues that from a US national-interest standpoint, resources should be pulled from the Middle East and redirected toward East Asia, since the US is now a net energy exporter and Persian Gulf oil is far less strategically important than it was in the 1970s.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Reinforces the 'pivot to Asia' thesis and implies continued Middle East military engagement is a strategic misallocation relative to the China challenge, relevant to defense-sector capital allocation and geopolitical risk weighting.
insights.quote: if you want to rejigger your forces, you should be getting out of the Middle East and shifting some resources towards bolstering your position in East Asia.

insights.claim: Kirshner states the recent US strikes on Iran depleted important munitions stockpiles that Asia specialists had specifically been building up for a possible military confrontation in the Asia-Pacific, directly weakening the US position vis-à-vis China.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term readiness signal: US munitions depletion in the Middle East is a direct drag on Indo-Pacific deterrence capacity, relevant to defense procurement and China-Taiwan risk assessments.
insights.quote: we have depleted many of our most important munitions in that effort.

insights.claim: Kirshner calls the recent US military action against Iran a 'bizarre choice' that is unexplainable by conventional IR theory or a coherent conception of the American national interest, given the US is now more resource-constrained relative to China than two decades ago.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Challenges any narrative that recent Iran strikes reflect coherent grand strategy; investors pricing in strategic rationality behind Middle East actions may be mistaken.
insights.quote: investing more in the Middle East again is a bizarre choice. It cannot be explained by any off-the-shelf IR theories.

themes.theme: Trump Foreign Policy as Personalist Kleptocracy

insights.claim: Kirshner, describing himself as 'a card carrying realist,' argues the current Trump administration's foreign policy is not realism by any definition but something closer to a 'personalist kleptocracy,' Stephen Walt's 'predatory hegemony,' or 'neo-royalism' — no existing IR theory adequately explains its behavior.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: For investors modeling US policy predictability, standard national-interest-based forecasting frameworks will likely fail; decisions are better explained by personal/financial motives than geopolitical strategy.
insights.quote: Trust me, as a card carrying realist, this is not realism by any stretch of the imagination.

insights.claim: Kirshner argues continued US engagement in the Middle East, though strategically irrational from a national-interest standpoint, makes more sense once reframed as serving the personal business interests of a 'kleptocratic' leadership rather than the country's actual interests.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Suggests weighting decision-makers' personal/financial conflicts of interest, not just stated policy, when forecasting US Middle East engagement and related energy/market risk.
insights.quote: if you start to think of the US foreign policy as run by a cotier of kleptocratic leaders, then the Middle East is a place where they have a lot of personal business interests.

insights.claim: Kirshner calls the current Trump administration 'the singularly most overtly corrupt presidential administration in American history,' citing the president's cryptocurrency ventures generating roughly a billion dollars in personal profit while in office.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: A specific, quantified conflict-of-interest data point for investors monitoring regulatory and reputational risk around White House-linked crypto ventures.
insights.quote: Would President Biden or Obama sell cryptocurrency from the White House and make a billion dollars doing it? I mean, these are beyond the imagination.

insights.claim: Kirshner notes the administration's coercive, 'might makes right' approach may be producing apparent results in Venezuela, but argues this template will not generalize to stronger actors since Venezuela is not a peer competitor.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: A caution against extrapolating tactical wins against weak states into a viable general strategy for dealing with stronger actors like Iran or China.
insights.quote: I think the track record on that is mixed. I mean, maybe it works in Venezuela. Venezuela is not what you'd call a peer competitor, but it's not going to work elsewhere.

themes.theme: Alliance Credibility Erosion and the Stakes in East Asia

insights.claim: Kirshner argues the US is 'torching' its alliance networks and will not easily undo the reputational damage, because the rest of the world must now update its baseline assumption of what the American political system can produce, regardless of who wins future elections.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Implies a structurally higher, sticky risk premium on US alliance reliability going forward, relevant to defense-spending trajectories in Europe/Asia and dollar/security-order-linked asset flows even after a change in administration.
insights.quote: everybody around the world has to understand that this is what the American political system can produce, especially with the re-election of Trump

insights.claim: Kirshner frames the central strategic question in East Asia as not whether China will invade its neighbors, but whether China's political and economic weight will make its regional dominance 'irresistible,' and whether states believe the US will remain an engaged partner if a crisis forces the issue.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Reframes China risk away from a binary invasion scenario toward a slower-moving credibility/bandwagoning dynamic; regional states' hedging decisions hinge on perceived US reliability, not just military balance.
insights.quote: is China's power going to mean that its political will will ultimately be irresistible and that China will come to politically dominate almost all of East Asia.

insights.claim: Kirshner warns the US is behaving 'like a bully' internationally, and that most bullies back down when confronted, raising genuine doubt among East Asian actors about whether the US would actually show up militarily if a regional war broke out.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: A direct warning that US extended-deterrence credibility in Asia is now in question, a key variable for pricing geopolitical risk in Taiwan-adjacent supply chains and regional defense budgets.
insights.quote: I think the US is behaving in the world like a bully and most bullies when confronted tend to back down.

insights.claim: Even in the recent Iran conflict, ostensibly fought on behalf of an ally, the US wavered repeatedly and was publicly at odds with the Israeli prime minister, illustrating that US unreliability extends even to its closest security partnerships, not just secondary alliances.
insights.speaker: Demetri Kofinas, host
insights.so_what: Signals that even 'special relationship' alliances are not immune to the credibility erosion described, relevant to assessing Israel-related and broader Middle East geopolitical risk premia.

themes.theme: Domestic Institutional Erosion and Executive Power

insights.claim: Kirshner contrasts the Watergate era, when institutional actors defended their prerogatives regardless of party (exemplified by Barry Goldwater telling Nixon to resign), with today's Congress, including a House Speaker who 'serves at the pleasure of the president,' which has largely ceded institutional authority to the executive branch.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Documents a structural weakening of US checks and balances, relevant to long-run assessments of US political and regulatory predictability that underpin sovereign credit and equity risk premia.
insights.quote: Today we have a speaker of the house who serves at the pleasure of the president.

insights.claim: Kirshner argues that a Supreme Court ruling granting the president broad immunity means many of the same acts Nixon was prosecuted for during Watergate would now be treated as lawful presidential conduct, representing a fundamental expansion of unconstrained executive power.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: A legal/structural shift that raises the ceiling on future unilateral executive action with fewer institutional checks, a factor to weight in political-risk modeling of US policy volatility.
insights.quote: if the Supreme Court had immunized the president in 1970, all of the crimes that Nixon committed during Watergate and they were crimes.

themes.theme: Wealth as a Gateway to Political Power

insights.claim: Kirshner observes that American business and bureaucratic elites, including billionaires who initially kept their distance from Trump, have largely aligned with the administration, and that 'every billionaire now wants to be a trillionaire,' reflecting an unprecedented drive to convert wealth into political power.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Signals elite capital increasingly aligning with executive power for access and protection rather than acting as an independent check, relevant to assessing regulatory-capture risk and durability of policy favoritism toward large incumbents.
insights.quote: every billionaire now wants to be a trillionaire. And this is mind-boggling to me.

insights.claim: Kirshner points to Martin Wolf's book 'The Crisis of Democratic Capitalism' as capturing how concentrated wealth increasingly captures political power in a way not possible 10-30 years ago, linking wealth concentration directly to the erosion of US democratic institutions discussed throughout the conversation.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Provides a citable macro-political framework connecting wealth concentration trends to institutional and governance risk, useful for governance-oriented investors tracking political-economy tail risks.
